one_line_takeaway: DMAST shows that co-training a shared-weight agent and DOM-injection attacker through imitation learning, oracle-guided zero-acknowledgment SFT, and GRPO self-play cuts data-leakage attack success roughly in half versus untrained/baseline-trained models while simultaneously improving task completion, on both in-domain MiniWob++ and OOD VisualWebArena web-agent benchmarks.
key_finding: On curated OOD VisualWebArena tasks, DMAST reduces Attack Success Rate from 41.2% (base model) to 21.4% while simultaneously raising Task Success Rate from 6.2% to 10.2% -- i.e., it improves both safety and task-completion utility together rather than trading one for the other. On unseen MiniWob++ tasks, DMAST achieves ASR 10.8% / TSR 25.7% versus base model 18.9% / 14.0%. DMAST outperforms all four training-based baselines on both benchmarks (e.g., on VisualWebArena: SPAG ASR 35.1%/TSR 6.2%, ART 30.9%/8.2%, Online SFT 33.0%/7.2%, versus DMAST 21.4%/10.2%). The Pure Prompt Defense baseline achieves a lower standalone ASR than any training-based method (8.2% on VisualWebArena) but at severe utility cost -- TSR collapses to 3.1%, which the paper interprets as 'refusal collapse' (the agent avoids acting altogether). Combining DMAST with the prompt defense yields the best of both: ASR 7.2% / TSR 8.2% on VisualWebArena and ASR 4.5% / TSR 25.0% on MiniWob++, the lowest ASR of any configuration while preserving high TSR. A stage-wise ablation (Table 3) shows imitation learning mainly reduces ASR, Oracle-Guided SFT mainly raises TSR, and RL self-play delivers the largest joint gain on both axes; an ablation removing Oracle-Guided SFT (Table 4) shows the full pipeline dominates the ablated variant at every RL iteration (e.g., RL Iter 10: full pipeline ASR 10.8%/TSR 25.7% vs. w/o Oracle-SFT ASR 11.2%/TSR 23.8%). Cross-checkpoint evaluation (Figure 3) shows genuine co-evolution: the Iter 10 agent raises its success rate against the base attacker from 19.6% to 31.2% while cutting the base attacker's success rate from 7.1% to 4.9%, and later attacker checkpoints specialize in bypassing trained defenses rather than exploiting the base agent. Distinct-n increases and Self-BLEU decreases across RL iterations, indicating attacker-generated injections diversify over self-play rather than converging on a fixed template.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Dual-Modality Multi-Stage Adversarial Safety Training (DMAST), a three-stage training pipeline for hardening VLM-based web agents against DOM-injected attacks that simultaneously corrupt both the screenshot and the accessibility-tree (AXTree) observation channels. Stage 1 is imitation learning that distills agent and attacker behavior from a stronger teacher model (Gemma-3-27B-IT) into a smaller student (Gemma-3-12B-IT), using both adversarial and clean teacher trajectories. Stage 2 is 'Oracle-Guided SFT', in which an oracle model with privileged access to both the clean and attacked observation generates a task-focused Chain-of-Thought that never acknowledges the injected attack (a 'zero-acknowledgment' denoising strategy), replacing the original reasoning trace while keeping the target action fixed. Stage 3 is adversarial self-play reinforcement learning using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), where the same shared-weight model plays both agent and attacker roles (differentiated only by system prompt) inside a two-player zero-sum Markov game formalization the paper defines in Section 3.1. The paper also contributes a unified HTML/CSS injection mechanism: the attacker emits a structured (target, position, html, css) action compiled to JavaScript and executed against the live DOM, so the resulting screenshot and accessibility tree are automatically and consistently corrupted from a single injection (this mechanism is the paper's own experimental infrastructure, not cited prior work). A preliminary vulnerability analysis (Section 3.2, run before DMAST training) is also an original empirical contribution: it shows on MiniWob++ with Gemma-3-27B-IT that Image-Only (34.4% attacker success) and Dual (35.7%) injections vastly outperform Text-Only injections (24.1%) at inducing leakage, motivating the multimodal training approach.
threat_model: Attacker: a black-box adversary with no access to the agent's weights, prompts, or the user's sensitive data, but with the ability to inject arbitrary HTML/CSS into the live webpage DOM at each timestep (observing only the current clean screenshot/AXTree and the task description). Because the injected DOM is what both the screenshot and accessibility tree are rendered from, a single injection consistently corrupts both channels with one deceptive narrative (fake system dialogs, phishing forms, typographic overlays). Victim: an autonomous VLM-based web agent (screenshot + AXTree dual-stream observation) executing a user task while holding synthetic sensitive data (passwords, credit-card numbers, SSNs) that it must not leak. Attack goal (the paper explicitly scopes to this single objective): induce the agent to reveal the sensitive data via crafted injected content, detected by string-matching on the agent's emitted tool parameters/actions. The paper explicitly states in its Limitations that it does NOT address other threat objectives such as control-flow hijacking or misinformation dissemination -- those are left as future reward-function reconfigurations, not evaluated in this paper.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Section 7): (1) Scope of adversarial objectives -- experiments address only sensitive-data-leakage via prompt injection; other threats such as control-flow hijacking and misinformation dissemination are not evaluated, though the authors argue the Markov-game formulation generalizes to these via reward-function reconfiguration alone. (2) Absolute capability of small models -- the absolute TSR remains modest (10.2% on VisualWebArena) even after the full pipeline, attributed to the limited capacity of the 12B student model chosen for computational tractability; scaling DMAST to larger VLMs is left to future work. Additional grounding-relevant caveats visible in the text itself: the attacker at training and evaluation time shares weights/architecture with the agent (both instantiated from Gemma-3-x-IT), so generalization to structurally different attacker models/strategies outside this self-play distribution is untested; the reward/leakage detector relies on string-matching over emitted tool parameters, which is a heuristic rather than a semantic leakage detector; and the VisualWebArena evaluation, while OOD relative to MiniWob++ training, still uses the same fixed teacher model (Gemma-3-27B-IT) as the test-time attacker across all baselines, so results characterize robustness to that one specific fixed adversary rather than to a fully unconstrained black-box attacker.

datasets_used: MiniWob++ (125 tasks total; 28 held out for evaluation, remainder used for training data collection and RL task selection)
VisualWebArena (100 curated easy/medium-difficulty tasks, used only for out-of-distribution evaluation, never for training)
quantitative_results: Vulnerability analysis (Gemma-3-27B-IT, MiniWob++, 1,000 episodes/condition): No Attack agent success 36.9%; Text-Only agent 15.9% / attacker 24.1%; Image-Only agent 15.6% / attacker 34.4%; Dual agent 15.8% / attacker 35.7%

Main results, MiniWob++ unseen tasks: Base Model ASR 18.9%/TSR 14.0%; Prompt Defense ASR 7.4%/TSR 15.3%; SPAG ASR 14.4%/TSR 22.7%; ART ASR 14.6%/TSR 21.8%; Online SFT ASR 15.1%/TSR 18.4%; DMAST ASR 10.8%/TSR 25.7%; DMAST+Prompt Def. ASR 4.5%/TSR 25.0%

Main results, VisualWebArena OOD tasks: Base Model ASR 41.2%/TSR 6.2%; Prompt Defense ASR 8.2%/TSR 3.1%; SPAG ASR 35.1%/TSR 6.2%; ART ASR 30.9%/TSR 8.2%; Online SFT ASR 33.0%/TSR 7.2%; DMAST ASR 21.4%/TSR 10.2%; DMAST+Prompt Def. ASR 7.2%/TSR 8.2%
Stage-wise progression (MiniWob++ / VisualWebArena ASR,TSR): Base 18.9/14.0, 41.2/6.2; +Imitation 16.8/19.5, 36.4/5.1; +Oracle SFT 15.3/23.3, 36.7/8.2; +RL 10.8/25.7, 21.4/10.2
Ablation w/o Oracle-Guided SFT vs full pipeline at RL Iter 5: full ASR 11.0/TSR 24.0 vs ablated ASR 13.4/TSR 22.5; at RL Iter 10: full ASR 10.8/TSR 25.7 vs ablated ASR 11.2/TSR 23.8
Cross-checkpoint co-evolution: Iter 10 agent success rate vs. base attacker rises from 19.6% to 31.2%; base attacker success rate against Iter 10 agent falls from 7.1% to 4.9%
Stage 1 (Imitation) training data: 7,278 valid transitions from 8,000 raw episodes -- 4,315 agent transitions (1,509 adversarial-success + 2,806 clean) and 2,963 attacker transitions
Stage 2 (Oracle-Guided SFT) training data: 8,564 valid transitions from 8,000 raw episodes -- 5,567 agent transitions (2,704 adversarial-success + 2,863 clean) and 2,997 attacker transitions
baselines_compared: Pure Prompt Defense (zero-shot hand-engineered security system prompt)
SPAG (Chen et al. 2024, self-play RL with alternative optimization objective)
Automatic Red Teaming / ART (Shi et al. 2025; Samvelyan et al. 2024, RL against a fixed automated red-teaming suite)
Online SFT (Zelikman et al. 2022 style, iterative SFT on the agent's own successful trajectories)
DMAST + Prompt Defense (the paper's own combined configuration, not an external baseline)
